lines.213: │  TABLET I — INTRODUCTION AND ENKIDU'S CREATION:                  │
lines.214: │  "He who saw the Deep" — narrator's prologue establishes         │
lines.215: │  Gilgamesh as 2/3 divine, 1/3 human; king of Uruk                │
lines.216: │  Problem: Gilgamesh is too vigorous — exhausts his people        │
lines.217: │  with labor and sexual demands (prima nocta?)                    │
lines.218: │  Aruru (creation goddess) creates Enkidu from clay + spit        │
lines.219: │  — Enkidu is "natural man": lives with animals, eats grass       │
lines.220: │  Gilgamesh hears of him; sends a sacred prostitute (Shamhat)     │
lines.221: │  Shamhat civilizes Enkidu through 6 days/7 nights of sex         │
lines.222: │  — Animals no longer know him; he has language and culture      │
lines.223: │  — The Sumerian theory of civilization: sex = entry to culture  │
lines.225: │  TABLETS II–III — GILGAMESH AND ENKIDU MEET AND BOND:            │
lines.226: │  Enkidu comes to Uruk; initially blocks Gilgamesh (stopping      │
lines.227: │  him from exercising prima nocta rights); they wrestle           │
lines.228: │  → Gilgamesh wins; they embrace; become inseparable friends      │
lines.229: │  Together they plan the Cedar Forest expedition                  │
lines.232: │  Journey to Cedar Forest (Lebanon); guarded by Humbaba           │
lines.233: │  Humbaba: monster appointed by Enlil to protect the cedars       │
lines.235: │  Shamash (sun god) helps them; they kill Humbaba                 │
lines.236: │  Enkidu urges killing; Gilgamesh hesitates (Humbaba begs mercy)  │
lines.237: │  They cut down the cedars and return with timber                 │
lines.239: │  TABLET VI — ISHTAR'S PROPOSAL; BULL OF HEAVEN:                  │
lines.240: │  Ishtar proposes marriage to Gilgamesh upon return               │
lines.241: │  GILGAMESH REFUSES — lists Ishtar's discarded lovers:            │
lines.242: │    Tammuz (the shepherd), the bird, the lion, the horse...       │
lines.243: │    all destroyed by her; he will not join them                   │
lines.244: │  Ishtar, furious, demands her father Anu send Bull of Heaven     │
lines.245: │  Bull descends; Enkidu grabs its horns; Gilgamesh kills it       │
lines.246: │  Gilgamesh mocks Ishtar: throws bull's haunch at her face        │
lines.247: │  Ishtar and the women of Uruk mourn; heroes celebrate            │
lines.250: │  The gods deliberate: killing Humbaba AND the Bull of Heaven     │
lines.251: │  demands divine retribution. Enlil decrees Enkidu must die.      │
lines.252: │  Enkidu falls ill; twelve days of sickness                       │
lines.253: │  Enkidu curses the prostitute Shamhat (she civilized him →       │
lines.254: │  led to this death); Shamash intervenes; he blesses her instead  │
lines.255: │  Enkidu dreams of the Underworld: the dead as birds in dust,     │
lines.256: │  kings dethroned, all equal in the great below                   │
lines.261: │  "Six days and seven nights I mourned over him                   │
lines.262: │   until a maggot dropped from his nostril"                       │
lines.265: │  TABLETS IX–X — THE QUEST FOR IMMORTALITY:                       │
lines.266: │  Gilgamesh sets out to find Utnapishtim (the only mortal to      │
lines.267: │  achieve immortality, granted by the gods after the flood)       │
lines.268: │  Journey through the Mountains of Mashu (twin peaks holding up   │
lines.269: │  sky); scorpion-men guard the pass; they let him through         │
lines.270: │  Twelve leagues of darkness; emerges into paradise garden        │
lines.271: │  Meets Siduri (tavern keeper / "wine-maker of the gods"):        │
lines.272: │    She advises: "When the gods created mankind, they allotted    │
lines.273: │    death to mankind, but life they retained in their own keeping.│
lines.274: │    As for you, Gilgamesh, let your belly be full, make merry    │
lines.275: │    each day... look upon the child who holds your hand, let your │
lines.276: │    wife delight in your embrace — for this too is the lot of man"│
lines.278: │  Gilgamesh refuses; finds Urshanabi (boatman of the dead)        │
lines.279: │  Crosses the Waters of Death; meets Utnapishtim                  │
lines.283: │  Utnapishtim says: "We granted you kingship; immortality we      │
lines.284: │  did not grant you." Offers Gilgamesh a test: stay awake for     │
lines.285: │  six days and seven nights. Gilgamesh fails (falls asleep).      │
lines.287: │  There is a plant at the bottom of the sea — "Old Man Grown      │
lines.290: │  Plans to bring it to Uruk to give to old men                    │
lines.291: │  Stops to bathe; a serpent steals the plant (sheds its skin)     │
lines.292: │  → The snake's skin-shedding explained; immortality lost         │
lines.296: │  Enkidu descends to the underworld to retrieve Gilgamesh's       │
lines.297: │  drum and drumstick; becomes trapped; his ghost returns to give  │
lines.298: │  Gilgamesh a report on the land of the dead                      │
lines.300: │  CLOSING: Gilgamesh returns to Uruk; shows Urshanabi the walls   │
lines.301: │  he built. The frame narrative: "Go up on the wall of Uruk...    │
lines.302: │  see how its foundations are laid... is not even the foundation  │
lines.303: │  of brick a thing of permanence?" → The answer to mortality      │

lines.213: │  TABLET I — INTRODUCTION AND ENKIDU'S CREATION:                  │
lines.214: │  "He who saw the Deep" — narrator's prologue establishes         │
lines.215: │  Gilgamesh as 2/3 divine, 1/3 human; king of Uruk                │
lines.216: │  Problem: Gilgamesh is too vigorous — exhausts his people        │
lines.217: │  with labor and sexual demands (prima nocta?)                    │
lines.218: │  Aruru (creation goddess) creates Enkidu from clay + spit        │
lines.219: │  — Enkidu is "natural man": lives with animals, eats grass       │
lines.220: │  Gilgamesh hears of him; sends a sacred prostitute (Shamhat)     │
lines.221: │  Shamhat civilizes Enkidu through 6 days/7 nights of sex         │
lines.222: │  — Animals no longer know him; he has language and culture      │
lines.223: │  — The Sumerian theory of civilization: sex = entry to culture  │
lines.225: │  TABLETS II–III — GILGAMESH AND ENKIDU MEET AND BOND:            │
lines.226: │  Enkidu comes to Uruk; initially blocks Gilgamesh (stopping      │
lines.227: │  him from exercising prima nocta rights); they wrestle           │
lines.228: │  → Gilgamesh wins; they embrace; become inseparable friends      │
lines.229: │  Together they plan the Cedar Forest expedition                  │
lines.232: │  Journey to Cedar Forest (Lebanon); guarded by Humbaba           │
lines.233: │  Humbaba: monster appointed by Enlil to protect the cedars       │
lines.235: │  Shamash (sun god) helps them; they kill Humbaba                 │
lines.236: │  Enkidu urges killing; Gilgamesh hesitates (Humbaba begs mercy)  │
lines.237: │  They cut down the cedars and return with timber                 │
lines.239: │  TABLET VI — ISHTAR'S PROPOSAL; BULL OF HEAVEN:                  │
lines.240: │  Ishtar proposes marriage to Gilgamesh upon return               │
lines.241: │  GILGAMESH REFUSES — lists Ishtar's discarded lovers:            │
lines.242: │    Tammuz (the shepherd), the bird, the lion, the horse...       │
lines.243: │    all destroyed by her; he will not join them                   │
lines.244: │  Ishtar, furious, demands her father Anu send Bull of Heaven     │
lines.245: │  Bull descends; Enkidu grabs its horns; Gilgamesh kills it       │
lines.246: │  Gilgamesh mocks Ishtar: throws bull's haunch at her face        │
lines.247: │  Ishtar and the women of Uruk mourn; heroes celebrate            │
lines.250: │  The gods deliberate: killing Humbaba AND the Bull of Heaven     │
lines.251: │  demands divine retribution. Enlil decrees Enkidu must die.      │
lines.252: │  Enkidu falls ill; twelve days of sickness                       │
lines.253: │  Enkidu curses the prostitute Shamhat (she civilized him →       │
lines.254: │  led to this death); Shamash intervenes; he blesses her instead  │
lines.255: │  Enkidu dreams of the Underworld: the dead as birds in dust,     │
lines.256: │  kings dethroned, all equal in the great below                   │
lines.261: │  "Six days and seven nights I mourned over him                   │
lines.262: │   until a maggot dropped from his nostril"                       │
lines.265: │  TABLETS IX–X — THE QUEST FOR IMMORTALITY:                       │
lines.266: │  Gilgamesh sets out to find Utnapishtim (the only mortal to      │
lines.267: │  achieve immortality, granted by the gods after the flood)       │
lines.268: │  Journey through the Mountains of Mashu (twin peaks holding up   │
lines.269: │  sky); scorpion-men guard the pass; they let him through         │
lines.270: │  Twelve leagues of darkness; emerges into paradise garden        │
lines.271: │  Meets Siduri (tavern keeper / "wine-maker of the gods"):        │
lines.272: │    She advises: "When the gods created mankind, they allotted    │
lines.273: │    death to mankind, but life they retained in their own keeping.│
lines.274: │    As for you, Gilgamesh, let your belly be full, make merry    │
lines.275: │    each day... look upon the child who holds your hand, let your │
lines.276: │    wife delight in your embrace — for this too is the lot of man"│
lines.278: │  Gilgamesh refuses; finds Urshanabi (boatman of the dead)        │
lines.279: │  Crosses the Waters of Death; meets Utnapishtim                  │
lines.283: │  Utnapishtim says: "We granted you kingship; immortality we      │
lines.284: │  did not grant you." Offers Gilgamesh a test: stay awake for     │
lines.285: │  six days and seven nights. Gilgamesh fails (falls asleep).      │
lines.287: │  There is a plant at the bottom of the sea — "Old Man Grown      │
lines.290: │  Plans to bring it to Uruk to give to old men                    │
lines.291: │  Stops to bathe; a serpent steals the plant (sheds its skin)     │
lines.292: │  → The snake's skin-shedding explained; immortality lost         │
lines.296: │  Enkidu descends to the underworld to retrieve Gilgamesh's       │
lines.297: │  drum and drumstick; becomes trapped; his ghost returns to give  │
lines.298: │  Gilgamesh a report on the land of the dead                      │
lines.300: │  CLOSING: Gilgamesh returns to Uruk; shows Urshanabi the walls   │
lines.301: │  he built. The frame narrative: "Go up on the wall of Uruk...    │
lines.302: │  see how its foundations are laid... is not even the foundation  │
lines.303: │  of brick a thing of permanence?" → The answer to mortality      │

lines.368: │  PREMISE: Inanna decides to descend to the Great Below          │
lines.369: │  (Kur / Irkalla) — ruled by her sister Ereshkigal               │
lines.371: │  — To attend the funeral rites of Ereshkigal's husband Gugalanna │
lines.372: │  — To conquer or understand the realm of death                  │
lines.373: │  — To reclaim something; her motives are enigmatic              │
lines.376: │  Inanna passes through 7 gates, surrendering one attribute each:│
lines.378: │  Gate 1: Crown → "Give me the crown." Neti (gatekeeper) removes │
lines.379: │  Gate 2: Lapis lazuli measuring rod and line                    │
lines.380: │  Gate 3: Lapis lazuli beads at her throat                       │
lines.381: │  Gate 4: Double strand of beads on her breast                   │
lines.382: │  Gate 5: Gold ring from her hand                                │
lines.383: │  Gate 6: Breastplate ("Come, woman, the jewels of a woman...")  │
lines.384: │  Gate 7: Royal robe ("Come, woman, all must be naked before     │
lines.385: │          the queen of the Great Below")                         │
lines.387: │  Inanna arrives naked and bowed low before Ereshkigal           │
lines.388: │  Ereshkigal commands the seven judges (Anunnaki): they fasten   │
lines.389: │  their eyes upon her — the look of death                        │
lines.390: │  The corpse of Inanna is hung on a peg                         │
lines.393: │  Inanna had given instructions to her servant Ninshubar:        │
lines.394: │  if she doesn't return in three days, mourn and plead for help  │
lines.396: │  Enki creates two beings from the dirt under his fingernail     │
lines.398: │  — They descend and comfort Ereshkigal (who is in labor-pain)  │
lines.400: │  — They ask for Inanna's corpse; sprinkle Water of Life and     │
lines.404: │  "No one ascends from the underworld without a substitute"       │
lines.405: │  Inanna must send someone to take her place                      │
lines.406: │  She travels with demons (galla) who escort the dead             │
lines.409: │  Inanna's servant Ninshubar is in mourning → spared             │
lines.410: │  Her sons are in mourning → spared                              │
lines.411: │  But Dumuzi (her husband, the shepherd god) is on his throne    │
lines.412: │  in fine clothes, not mourning at all                           │
lines.413: │  Inanna "fastens on him the eye of death" → Dumuzi taken down   │
lines.414: │  (Dumuzi's sister Geshtinanna bargains: each takes six months)  │
lines.415: │  → Seasonal cycle of vegetation: Dumuzi below = winter/drought  │
lines.418: │  — Psychological: stripping of identity, confronting death,     │
lines.420: │  — Seasonal myth: vegetation god dying and returning            │
lines.421: │  — Political: Inanna/Ishtar's dual nature (love + war) explained│
